source_metadata.title_raw: Nicole C. Wright - A-19-199 - October 3, 2019 - Mountain View

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Nicole C. Wright 
Assistant City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
City of Mountain View 
500 Castro Street, Post Office 7540 
Mountain View, CA 94039-7540 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-199 
Dear Ms. Wright: 
October 3, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Mountain View City 
Councilmember Alison Hicks regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform 
Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a 
fin4er of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we 
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hicks from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed office development project given that the Councilmember owns a single-
family home located approximately 700 feet from the project? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the 
project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions may have a material financial effect 
on her real property interest in her single-family home located approximately 700 feet from the 
project. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-199 
Page No. 2 
You are an Assistant City Attorney for the City of Mountain View and the authorized 
representative of Mountain View City Councilmember Alison Hicks. The Mountain View City 
Council will soon consider governmental decisions relating to a proposed office development 
project (the "Project") on a 0.95-acre parcel located at 590 Castro Street (the "Project Site"), at the 
comer of Castro Street and Church Street, in the City's downtown. The Project would demolish an 
existing 9,228 square foot, single-story Wells Fargo bank building and associated surface parking 
lot in order to construct a 105,812 or 117,607 square foot, four-story office building with an 
underground parking lot. The Project would include a paseo from Castro Street to Eagle Park, a 
local community park northwest of the Project. 
The Councilmember has an interest worth $2,000 or more in a two-story single-family home 
located approximately 700 feet, or nearly two city blocks, southeast of the Project Site on Church 
Street near its intersection with View Street in the City's Old Mountain View Neighborhood. 
Church Street is lined by one- and two-story single-family homes in the immediate vicinity of the 
Councilmember's home, but there are multi-story buildings on Church Street approaching its 
intersection with Castro Street. The Councilmember's home faces Church Street, and those multi-
story buildings between her home and the Project Site appear to obstruct the view of the Project 
from her home. 
The Old Mountain View Neighborhood is made up of one- and two-story single-family 
homes on small lots, and includes a vibrant commercial area surrounding Castro Street made up of 
offices, shops, and restaurants. You state that Castro Street is the main commercial street in the 
City's downtown, vehicle traffic is often heavy on Castro Street, and some vehicle traffic uses Old 
Mountain View Neighborhood streets as shortcuts and cut-throughs. Castro Street is one lane each 
way, and has street parking on both sides. Church Street is one lane each way, but does not have 
street parking in the immediate vicinity of the Project Site. View Street is one lane each way with 
street parking on both sides. 
The existing surface parking lot currently located at the Project Site provides 63 parking 
spaces, and the Project would include three levels of underground parking with 291 to 353 parking 
spaces depending on the Project's final configuration. You state that there is already high demand 
for street parking in the area surrounding the Project Site·and in the City's downtown generally, 
some future visitors to the Project will not be able to use its underground parking, and it is possible 
that the Project will impact parking in the area surrounding the Project Site. 
The Councilmember's home is already subject to significant privacy intrusions and noise 
pollution due to being located nearby·substantial commercial activity on and around Castro Street. 
You state that any privacy, air-quality, or noise-level impacts on the Councilmember's home due to 
the Project would be minimal. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a-material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial .interests distinguishable from the 


File No. A-19-199 
Page No. 3 
decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that 
may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. 
With respect to decisions relating to the Project, the Councilmember has the following interests 
based on the facts presented: 
• 
A real property interest in her single-family home located in the City's Old Mountain View 
neighborhood because she has an interest in that real property worth $2,000 or more. (See 
Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in her personal finances and those of immediate family because an official always 
has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family. (See Section 
87103.) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the 
·subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the interest. In addition, an official's business interest is explicitly involved in any 
decision affecting the business as described in Regulation 18702.1 (a)(l ), and an official's real 
property interest is explicitly involved in any decision affecting that real property as described in 
Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect 
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the 
decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
The Councilmember's real property interest in her single-family home within the City's Old 
Mountain View Neighborhood is not explicitly involved in decisions relating to the Project. 
Therefore, those decisions' financial effect on the Councilmember's interest in that real property is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest, and subdivision (a)(8)(D) of that 
regulation provides that the effect is material if the decision: (1) involves property located more 
than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of the official's parcel, and (2) would 
change the parcel's character by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, 
privacy, noise levels, or air quality. 
The Councilmember's single-family home is approximately 700 feet, or just less than two 
city blocks, southeast of the Project Site. Decisions relating to the.Project may result in the 
replacement of a 9,228 square foot bank and a 63-space surface parking lot currently located at the 
Project Site with a 105,812 or 117,607 square foot office building and a 291 to 353 parking space 


File No. A-19-199 
Page No. 4 
underground parking lot. If approved, the Project would result in over ten times the existing 
commercial square footage, and over four times the existing parking spaces, at the Project Site. 
Therefore, it appears that decisions relating to the Project would change the character of the 
Councilmember's home by substantially altering the intensity of use of the area surrounding the 
Project Site based on the facts presented. 
You state that Castro Street is the main commercial street in the City's downtown, vehicle 
traffic is often heavy on Castro Street, and some vehicle traffic uses Old Mountain View 
Neighborhood streets as shortcuts and cut-throughs. Castro Street, Church Street, and View Street 
are all one lane each way. As noted above, if approved, the Project would result in four times the 
existing parking spaces at the Project Site, indicating that a significant number of additional 
vehicles will use those streets to go to and from the Project. 
Furthermore, the influx of additional office workers from the Project would substantially 
increase pedestrian traffic in the area surrounding of the Project, including the pedestrian traffic 
experienced on Church Street in front of the Councilmember's home. Also, the Project's paseo 
from Castro Street to Eagle Park may draw additional pedestrians from south and east of the 
Councilmember's home to the Project Site, further increasing pedestrian traffic around the 
Councilmember's home. Therefore, it appears that decisions relating to the Project would change 
the character of the Councilmember's home by substantially alter the traffic levels experienced at 
the Councilmember's home based on the facts presented. 
You state that there is already high demand for street parking in the area surrounding the 
Project Site and in the City's downtown generally, some future visitors to the Project will not be 
able to use its underground parking, and it is possible that the Project will impact parking in the area 
surrounding the Project Site. Castro Street has street parking on both sides, but Church street does 
not have street parking in the immediate vicinity of the Project Site. There is street parking on both 
sides of Church Street in front of the Councilmember's home, and on both sides of View Street 
nearby the Councilmember's home, and office workers and visitors to the Project are likely to seek 
out nearby street parking when there is insufficient on-site parking at the Project Site. Therefore, it 
appears that decisions relating to the Project would change the character of the Councilmember's 
home by substantially altering demand for street parking nearby. 
Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating to the Project would have a material 
financial effect on the Councilmember's real property interest in her single-family home because 
those decisions may change the character of that parcel by substantially altering its traffic levels, 
intensity of use, and parking under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8)(D).2 Accordingly, the Act prohibits 
the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions. 
2 Based on the facts presented, it does not appear that decisions relating·to the Project would affect the view 
from the Councilmember's parcel because multi-story buildings located between the Councilmember's home and the 
Project Site obstruct the view of the Project from her home. And you state that any privacy, noise-level, or air-quality 
impacts on the Co,uncilmember's home due to the Project would be minimal. Nonetheless, those decisions' reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on the Councilmember's home would be material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8)(D). 
Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the Act's materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's 
interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family, and subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that 
materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.2 if the decision will affect a real property in which the official 
has an interest. Because we have already determined that the Councilmember has a disqualifying conflict of interest 


File No. A-19-199 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this ma~ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
under Regulation 18702,2(a)(8)(D), we do not further analyze the effect of decisions relating to the Project on the 
Councilmember's interest in her personal finances or those of immediate family members.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hicks from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed office development project given that the Councilmember owns a single-
family home located approximately 700 feet from the project?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the 
project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions may have a material financial effect 
on her real property interest in her single-family home located approximately 700 feet from the 
project.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hicks from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed office development project given that the Councilmember owns a single-
family home located approximately 700 feet from the project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the 
project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions may have a material financial effect 
on her real property interest in her single-family home located approximately 700 feet from the 
project.
